text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 12 October 2025 

Dr A. Patel  
Hampstead Road Health Centre  
145 Hampstead Road, London NW1 3EE  

Dear Dr A. Patel,  

James Turner, DOB: 22-07-1983, Hospital No: J834920 NHS No. 1065937428  
Flat 3, 28 Larkspur Court, London, W1T 5PD 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with ongoing follow‑up; history of head injury in youth; mild asthma; vitamin D insufficiency (on supplementation); non-smoker; does not drink alcohol. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Salbutamol inhaler PRN.  
Colecalciferol 800 IU daily.  

I reviewed James Turner in clinic today, accompanied by his partner who helps with observations between appointments. He has been well in himself, working part-time and remaining physically active. Sleep has improved with a regular schedule. Appetite and weight are stable. 

Seizures: He keeps a detailed diary and notes occasional mood change and irritability for several hours prior to some events, which he recognises as a possible early warning and has started to track consistently. He wears a wrist-based seizure-alert device linked to his phone. Over the past six months, the device analytics have not detected any convulsive activity, and his personal event log corroborates this, with no recorded events requiring rescue measures. He last recalls a brief episode of confusion without collapse in late March, without injury. There have been no emergency attendances, no nocturnal events witnessed by his partner, and no missed doses reported. 

Examination: Alert and oriented, speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Tone and power normal in all limbs. Gait steady. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. 

Investigations: Blood tests from August 2025 showed FBC, U&Es, LFTs within normal limits; vitamin D now adequate at 74 nmol/L. Lamotrigine level within expected therapeutic range (measured for reference in shared-care). MRI brain from 2023 unchanged; routine EEG in 2024 non-specific. 

Plan: 
- Continue current anti-seizure medications unchanged.  
- Encourage ongoing diary tracking of prodromal mood/irritability and any associated triggers (sleep loss, stress, intercurrent illness).  
- Safety advice reinforced; he and his partner understand when to seek urgent help.  
- Annual blood monitoring to continue via GP (FBC, U&Es, LFTs; vitamin D in winter).  
- Review in clinic in 6–9 months, or sooner if events recur or device alerts suggest change. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past six months, the device analytics have not detected any convulsive activity, and his personal event log corroborates this, with no recorded events requiring rescue measures.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter discusses seizures explicitly. It states: over the past six months, device analytics have not detected any convulsive activity, corroborated by his log with no recorded events requiring rescue measures. It also mentions a last brief episode of confusion without collapse in late March. Clinic date is 12 October 2025. Late March to October is over 6 months. The documented absence of convulsive activity/events over the past six months supports seizure freedom for at least 6 months. Although there was a brief episode of confusion in late March, it predates the six-month seizure-free window and there have been no events since. Therefore, the current status is seizure free for multiple months (at least six).
seizure_frequency_number: Over the past six months, the device analytics have not detected any convulsive activity, and his personal event log corroborates this, with no recorded events requiring rescue measures.
